role_summary: Mississippi House of Representatives member 1968-2004 (47th district, Holmes County); the first African American elected to the Mississippi State Legislature since 1894 (the Reconstruction-era cutoff); the longest-continuously-serving member of the Mississippi House at his 2003 retirement; the first African American committee chairman in the chamber (Education Committee, 1977); Speaker Pro Tempore 1992-2004.
ai_reading: Clark's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a cosine count of 183; the broader cluster includes Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34, SNCC Washington and Carmichael memoir co-author), Walter Bruce (entry 123), and Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. (entry 39, the parallel-trajectory South Carolina Supreme Court chief justice). The McLaurin cosine is the catalog's clearest example of a Mississippi-legislator-and-Mississippi-organizer cross-cohort pairing: Clark's 36 years as a state legislator and McLaurin's voter-registration field work in Sunflower County overlap in geography and constituency. The Finney pairing is the parallel: both men entered their states' previously all-white legislative or judicial institutions in the late 1960s as the first since Reconstruction. The novel observation his page surfaces is the electoral-vs-direct-action distinction: Clark's institutional integration of the Mississippi legislature was a sustained 1968-2004 electoral arc, fundamentally different from the direct-action register of the catalog's SNCC and SCLC voices, yet the embedding finds his testimony clustering with the direct-action organizers rather than with civic-institution administrators, suggesting the corpus reads the Black-first-in-historically-all-white-Mississippi-institution work as continuous with the SNCC field tradition that preceded it.
biographical_paragraph: While Clark's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), his external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1]. Clark was born October 3, 1928 in Ebenezer, Holmes County, Mississippi, to a landowning family; his great-grandfather had first bought the land after the Civil War and his father Robert farmed it across the early twentieth century [src: 2]. He earned a Bachelor's at Jackson State University and a Master's in Administration and Educational Services from Michigan State University, completing nearly all of his doctoral work before entering politics. He worked as an educator and school administrator in Holmes County across the late 1950s and 1960s, the period when Holmes County emerged as one of the foundational Mississippi voter-registration counties (the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party did substantial work there in 1964). In 1967 he was elected to the Mississippi House of Representatives by the Black majority of Holmes County; he took his seat on January 2, 1968 as the first African American in the chamber since 1894 and remained the only African American representative in the state house through 1976 [src: 2]. In 1977 he became the first African American committee chairman in the Mississippi House, taking the Education Committee chairmanship he would hold for ten years across pivotal education-reform negotiations. He was elected Speaker Pro Tempore at the start of the 1992 session and re-elected to that position in 1996 and 2000. He retired in December 2003 as the longest-continuously-serving member of the Mississippi House. In 2004 he became the first African American to have a Mississippi state building named after him [src: 2]. He died March 4, 2025 at ninety-six [src: 2].
